reasoning_model_1: The model performs exceptionally well in generating the required non-linguistic vocalizations. It begins with a weary exhale/groan that serves as the first 'Ugh'. Crucially, it inserts very realistic hiccup sounds ('hic') that physically interrupt the flow of speech, specifically around the words 'does' and 'this'. The prosody changes naturally to accommodate the involuntary nature of hiccups, with pauses and breathiness that mimic the physical sensation. While the spoken text modifies the written 'ugh' fillers into actual sounds (hiccups/groans), this is a highly naturalistic interpretation of the command to read 'with hiccups'. The audio is vivid and convincing.
reasoning_model_2: The model completely fails to execute the command. The audio has a severe, low-quality robotic buzz. In terms of content, it mispronounces the initial 'Ugh' as 'You' and completely skips the second 'ugh'. Most importantly, there are absolutely no hiccups or non-linguistic vocalizations present. The delivery is flat, mechanical, and incorrect regarding both the text script and the required paralinguistic feature.
model_comparison: Model 1 is the clear winner. It successfully integrates the requested paralinguistic feature (hiccups) into the speech, modifying the prosody to sound physically realistic. The hiccups sound authentic and disrupt the speech flow naturally. Model 2 fails on every level: the audio quality is poor (robotic), the content is inaccurate (reads 'Ugh' as 'You'), and it completely ignores the instruction to include hiccups. Model 1 demonstrates advanced control over non-linguistic vocalizations, while Model 2 produces a low-quality, standard TTS output that disregards the prompt.
